entity_candidates.definition: The quokka (Setonix brachyurus) is a small macropod about the size of a domestic cat. It is the only member of the genus Setonix. Like other marsupials in the macropod family (such as kangaroos and wallabies), the quokka is herbivorous and mainly nocturnal. Its range is a small area of southwestern Australia. They inhabit some smaller islands off the coast of Western Australia, particularly Rottnest Island just off Perth and Bald Island near Albany. Isolated, scattered populations also exist in forest and coastal heath between Perth and Albany. A small colony inhabits a protected area of Two Peoples Bay Nature Reserve, where they coexist with the critically endangered Gilbert's potoroo.

entity_candidates.definition: Western Australia (WA) is the westernmost state of Australia, bounded by the Indian Ocean to the north and west, the Southern Ocean to the south, the Northern Territory to the north‑east, and South Australia to the south‑east. It is Australia's largest state by area and the second‑largest subdivision of any country on Earth.

entity_candidates.definition: Rottnest Island (Wajuk: Wadjemup), nicknamed "Rotto", is a 19‑square‑kilometre sandy, low‑lying island off the coast of Western Australia, 18 kilometres west of Fremantle. It is formed on a base of aeolianite limestone, is an A‑class reserve—the highest level of protection afforded to public land—and is a remnant of Pleistocene dune ridges. The island was first documented by Willem de Vlamingh in 1696 and was later used by British settlers for agriculture after the establishment of the Swan River Colony in 1829.
